title: Asymptotic Replacement for Quantum Channel Products with Applications to Inhomogeneous Matrix Product States
abstract: We develop a product-level trace-Dobrushin theory for finite-dimensional quantum channel products and apply it to deterministic and stationary random inhomogeneous matrix product states in left-canonical CPTP gauge. For a product of channels, the centered trace-Dobrushin coefficient quantifies the residual dependence on the input state, and its decay is the criterion for trace-norm forgetting. In the deterministic setting, this decay is equivalent to asymptotic replacement by a moving replacement channel. For two-sided products, pullback forgetting produces a unique boundary state, which determines the canonical replacement family. For stationary random CPTP cocycles, submultiplicativity of the product coefficient yields a trace-Dobrushin Lyapunov exponent. We prove that the almost sure negativity of this exponent is equivalent to quenched trace-norm memory loss and gives exponential forward and pullback convergence to a unique dynamically stationary random replacement channel. When the \(\varrho\)-mixing profile of the channel environment tends to zero, we obtain annealed super-polynomial estimates, while independence gives annealed exponential estimates. Finally, we transfer these estimates to inhomogeneous matrix product states whose auxiliary transfer maps are CPTP. These channel estimates transfer to deterministic and stationary random inhomogeneous MPS, giving infinite-volume limits of trace-closed finite-volume states, quantitative boundary stability, and correlation bounds governed by the same auxiliary product coefficients.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous mathematical framework for understanding how sequences of different quantum channels forget their initial states. It proves that the decay of a specific contraction coefficient determines whether channels converge to a replacement family, and shows how these dynamics translate to the stability and correlation decay in random Matrix Product States.
main_problem: Investigating the asymptotic behavior and memory loss of products of non-identical (inhomogeneous) and random quantum channels, and applying these results to the stability and correlation decay of Matrix Product States (MPS).
main_result: The paper establishes that the decay of the trace-Dobrushin coefficient is equivalent to the asymptotic replacement of channels by a moving replacement family, and proves that a negative Lyapunov exponent leads to exponential convergence and spatial clustering in random MPS.
method: The authors develop a product-level trace-Dobrushin theory using contraction coefficients, ergodic theory (Lyapunov exponents), and transfer map formalism to analyze both deterministic and random CPTP cocycles.
model_or_system: The study focuses on finite-dimensional Completely Positive Trace-Preserving (CPTP) quantum channels, specifically sequences of inhomogeneous channels and stationary random quantum cocycles, with applications to inhomogeneous Matrix Product States (MPS) in the left-canonical gauge.
key_observables: Trace-norm distance, trace-Dobrushin coefficient, trace-Dobrushin Lyapunov exponent, and connected correlation functions of local observables.
important_parameters: Trace-Dobrushin coefficient (kappa_tr), Trace-Dobrushin Lyapunov exponent (lambda_tr), and the rho-mixing profile of the channel environment.
main_assumptions: The theory assumes finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, CPTP maps, and specifically for the MPS application, the left-canonical gauge; random results require a negative Lyapunov exponent almost surely.
paper_structure: The paper begins by developing a product-level trace-Dobrushin theory for deterministic channels, extends these results to two-sided products and pullback convergence, then moves to random quantum cocycles using ergodic theory, and concludes by transferring these estimates to the study of inhomogeneous and random Matrix Product States.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides rigorous quantitative bounds on how environmental noise and time-dependent dynamics lead to the loss of initial state memory and the emergence of unique steady-state behaviors in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
